content.text: AI Disclosure and Regulatory Reporting Requirements

Regulatory disclosure obligations for AI systems are expanding across every major jurisdiction. What began as voluntary model cards and transparency reports has matured into legally mandated conformity assessments (EU AI Act), material-event reporting to securities regulators (SEC), adverse-action notices under consumer protection law (ECOA/CFPB), and annual cybersecurity attestations to financial regulators (NYDFS). Organisations deploying GenAI at enterprise scale must now maintain a disclosure programme that is as operationally rigorous as their financial reporting processes.

This sub-subtopic maps the key disclosure obligations by regulatory regime, defines a materiality assessment framework for AI-related events, and provides code-level tooling to track, draft, and file disclosures within their regulatory deadlines.

The diagram illustrates four major regulatory clusters and four disclosure timing patterns. The disclosure obligations are not mutually exclusive — a US-listed financial services firm operating in the EU may simultaneously face SEC 10-K AI risk disclosures, NYDFS annual attestation, EU AI Act high-risk system registration, and CFPB adverse-action notice obligations. A unified disclosure tracker is the only practical way to manage the cross-regulatory calendar.
EU AI Act: High-Risk and GPAI Obligations

The EU AI Act Annex III lists eight sectors where AI systems are presumptively high-risk: biometric identification, critical infrastructure, education, employment, essential private services (credit, insurance), law enforcement, migration/asylum, and administration of justice. GenAI systems used in credit underwriting, medical diagnosis support, or hiring screening fall into this category.

content: Before deployment, high-risk systems must undergo a conformity assessment. For most Annex III systems, this is a self-assessment documented in technical documentation. For biometric identification and law enforcement, third-party assessment is required.

High-risk systems must be registered in the EU AI Office's public database before market placement. The registration includes system description, intended purpose, capabilities, limitations, and the deployer's identity.
Deployers must implement a post-market monitoring plan and report serious incidents to market surveillance authorities within 15 days of becoming aware.
Public bodies and private operators of critical infrastructure must conduct a FRIA before deploying high-risk AI that significantly affects persons.

content.text: AI models trained on more than 10²⁵ FLOPs (classified as systemic-risk GPAI) face enhanced obligations regardless of the specific application:
All GPAI models placed on EU market
SEC Disclosure Requirements for US Public Companies

The SEC's existing disclosure framework requires material risks to be disclosed in 10-K filings. Since 2023, SEC staff have increasingly issued comment letters asking companies to expand AI risk factor disclosures to address:

content: If the business model materially depends on AI systems, describe the nature of that dependence and what happens if the AI underperforms or becomes unavailable.
Under the SEC's 2023 cybersecurity disclosure rule, material AI-related cybersecurity risks (model theft, adversarial attacks, data poisoning) must be described.
Emerging AI regulation (EU AI Act, proposed US legislation) that could materially affect operations or require significant compliance investment.
If AI is used in consumer-facing decisions (credit, insurance, hiring), describe the risk of discriminatory outcomes and the controls in place.

content.text: The SEC cybersecurity rule (Item 1.05 of Form 8-K) requires disclosure of a material cybersecurity incident within four business days of
materiality — not of discovering the incident. A GenAI-related breach (training data exfiltration, adversarial attack causing erroneous outputs affecting customers) can trigger this obligation.
Form ADV: Investment Adviser AI Disclosures
Investment advisers using AI (including LLMs) in portfolio management, client recommendations, or research must disclose this in Form ADV Part 2A brochure under the fiduciary duty framework:

content: What AI tools are used and for what decisions — market research, client suitability scoring, trade execution optimisation.
If the adviser's proprietary AI model creates a conflict (e.g. recommends securities that benefit the adviser), this must be disclosed.
Known limitations of the AI models used, including their training data cutoff, accuracy under novel market conditions, and the risk of model drift.
How human advisers review and override AI recommendations before client-facing actions.

content.text: The Federal Reserve's SR 11-7 guidance on Model Risk Management applies to LLMs used in any decision-making role in banking: credit underwriting, fraud detection, AML, stress testing, or customer communication. LLMs are models under this framework and require:

content: Every LLM in production must be inventoried with its purpose, owner, validation status, and materiality tier.
Material models must be validated by a team independent of development. For LLMs, this includes evaluating training data quality, output accuracy, bias, and stability.
Performance metrics must be tracked over time. Model drift — degradation of accuracy or calibration — must trigger review and potential re-validation.
Banks must be able to produce model documentation, validation reports, and monitoring results to Federal Reserve or OCC examiners on request.

content.text: Consumer Protection: ECOA Adverse Action Notices

When an AI system makes or materially contributes to an adverse credit decision (denial, increased rate, reduced limit), the Equal Credit Opportunity Act and Regulation B require a specific adverse action notice. The CFPB has clarified that the complexity of an AI model does not excuse the obligation to provide specific reasons.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class AdverseActionReason(str, Enum):
    # Standard Regulation B reasons (model must map to one of these or similar)
    INSUFFICIENT_INCOME            = "Insufficient income"
    EXCESSIVE_OBLIGATIONS          = "Excessive obligations in relation to income"
    DELINQUENT_CREDIT_HISTORY      = "Delinquent past or present credit obligations"
    INSUFFICIENT_CREDIT_FILE       = "Insufficient credit file"
    UNABLE_TO_VERIFY_CREDIT        = "Unable to verify credit references"
    TEMPORARY_OR_IRREGULAR_INCOME  = "Temporary or irregular income"
    LENGTH_OF_EMPLOYMENT           = "Length of employment"
    UNACCEPTABLE_TYPE_OF_CREDIT    = "Unacceptable type of credit references"
    # AI-derived reasons must still map to specific, human-understandable factors
    MODEL_DERIVED_PAYMENT_PATTERN  = "Pattern of late payments in recent 12 months"
    MODEL_DERIVED_UTILISATION      = "High proportion of available credit in use"

@dataclass
class AdverseActionNotice:
    applicant_name: str
    applicant_address: str
    decision_date: str
    creditor_name: str
    # ECOA: up to 4 principal reasons required (not generic AI output)
    reasons: list[AdverseActionReason]
    ecoa_notice: str = (
        "The federal Equal Credit Opportunity Act prohibits creditors from "
        "discriminating against credit applicants on the basis of race, color, "
        "religion, national origin, sex, marital status, age (provided the "
        "applicant has the capacity to enter into a binding contract), or because "
        "all or part of the applicant's income derives from any public assistance "
        "program, or because the applicant has in good faith exercised any right "
        "under the Consumer Credit Protection Act."
    )

    def render_reasons_text(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"{i+1}. {r.value}" for i, r in enumerate(self.reasons[:4])]
        return "\n".join(lines)

class AIDecisionExplainer:
    """
    Converts AI model feature importances into Reg B-compliant reason statements.
    The model must output feature names and importances; this class maps them
    to approved reason codes.
    """
    _FEATURE_TO_REASON: dict[str, AdverseActionReason] = {
        "late_payment_count_12m":     AdverseActionReason.MODEL_DERIVED_PAYMENT_PATTERN,
        "credit_utilisation_ratio":   AdverseActionReason.MODEL_DERIVED_UTILISATION,
        "income_to_obligation_ratio":  AdverseActionReason.EXCESSIVE_OBLIGATIONS,
        "employment_months":          AdverseActionReason.LENGTH_OF_EMPLOYMENT,
        "num_credit_inquiries":       AdverseActionReason.INSUFFICIENT_CREDIT_FILE,
    }

    def top_adverse_reasons(
        self, feature_importances: dict[str, float], n: int = 4
    ) -> list[AdverseActionReason]:
        """
        Take the top-N features by importance (where importance is negative,
        i.e. harmed the application score), map to Reg B reasons.
        """
        sorted_features = sorted(
            feature_importances.items(), key=lambda x: x[1]  # ascending = most adverse first
        )
        reasons = []
        for feature, _ in sorted_features:
            reason = self._FEATURE_TO_REASON.get(feature)
            if reason and reason not in reasons:
                reasons.append(reason)
            if len(reasons) >= n:
                break
        return reasons

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta
from enum import Enum
import uuid

class DisclosureStatus(str, Enum):
    PENDING    = "pending"
    DRAFTED    = "drafted"
    REVIEWED   = "reviewed"
    FILED      = "filed"
    OVERDUE    = "overdue"

class DisclosureRegime(str, Enum):
    SEC_8K         = "sec_8k"
    SEC_10K        = "sec_10k"
    SEC_10Q        = "sec_10q"
    EU_AI_ACT      = "eu_ai_act"
    NYDFS_500      = "nydfs_500"
    CFPB_ECOA      = "cfpb_ecoa"
    FCA_DISC       = "fca"
    INTERNAL_BOARD = "internal_board"

@dataclass
class DisclosureObligation:
    id: str = field(default_factory=lambda: str(uuid.uuid4())[:8])
    regime: DisclosureRegime = DisclosureRegime.INTERNAL_BOARD
    description: str = ""
    trigger_event: str = ""
    due_date: date = field(default_factory=date.today)
    status: DisclosureStatus = DisclosureStatus.PENDING
    owner: str = ""
    filed_date: date | None = None
    notes: str = ""

    @property
    def is_overdue(self) -> bool:
        if self.status == DisclosureStatus.FILED:
            return False
        return date.today() > self.due_date

    @property
    def days_until_due(self) -> int:
        return (self.due_date - date.today()).days

class DisclosureCalendar:
    def __init__(self):
        self._obligations: list[DisclosureObligation] = []

    def add(self, obligation: DisclosureObligation) -> None:
        self._obligations.append(obligation)

    def overdue(self) -> list[DisclosureObligation]:
        return [o for o in self._obligations if o.is_overdue]

    def due_within(self, days: int) -> list[DisclosureObligation]:
        return [
            o for o in self._obligations
            if 0 <= o.days_until_due <= days and o.status != DisclosureStatus.FILED
        ]

    def standing_calendar(self, year: int) -> list[DisclosureObligation]:
        """Create standard annual disclosure obligations for a US-listed financial firm.""""
        standing = [
            DisclosureObligation(
                regime=DisclosureRegime.SEC_10K,
                description="Annual report — AI risk factors section",
                trigger_event="fiscal_year_end",
                due_date=date(year, 3, 31),   # 90 days after Dec 31 FY end (accelerated filer)
                owner="legal_counsel",
            ),
            DisclosureObligation(
                regime=DisclosureRegime.NYDFS_500,
                description="Annual cybersecurity certification — AI systems in scope",
                trigger_event="annual_cadence",
                due_date=date(year, 2, 15),
                owner="ciso",
            ),
            DisclosureObligation(
                regime=DisclosureRegime.EU_AI_ACT,
                description="High-risk AI system annual monitoring report",
                trigger_event="annual_cadence",
                due_date=date(year, 6, 30),
                owner="ai_governance_officer",
            ),
        ]
        self._obligations.extend(standing)
        return standing

    def register_incident(
        self, description: str, discovery_date: date, regime: DisclosureRegime
    ) -> DisclosureObligation:
        """Register an event-driven disclosure obligation with computed deadline.""""
        deadlines = {
            DisclosureRegime.SEC_8K:    discovery_date + timedelta(days=4),     # 4 business days
            DisclosureRegime.NYDFS_500: discovery_date + timedelta(hours=72),   # 72 hours
            DisclosureRegime.EU_AI_ACT: discovery_date + timedelta(days=15),    # 15 days
        }
        due = deadlines.get(regime, discovery_date + timedelta(days=30))
        obligation = DisclosureObligation(
            regime=regime,
            description=description,
            trigger_event="incident",
            due_date=due,
            owner="legal_counsel",
        )
        self._obligations.append(obligation)
        return obligation
Model Card and System Card as Disclosure Artefacts

Model cards (introduced by Google in 2018) and the EU AI Act's required technical documentation serve an overlapping function: they document intended use, performance characteristics, limitations, and risk mitigations of an AI system. For regulated entities, these are not optional communications — they form part of the evidence package for regulatory examination, conformity assessment, and shareholder disclosure.
Minimum Model Card Content for Regulatory Purposes
Architecture, training data summary, version, deployment date
EU AI Act Tech Doc, SR 11-7 inventory
Approved use cases, prohibited use cases, target population
EU AI Act Annex IV, CFPB fair lending
Accuracy, F1, AUC, calibration by demographic group
Known accuracy degradation conditions, adversarial robustness
EU AI Act Art. 13, SEC risk factors
Protected class performance parity, dataset representation
ECOA, CFPB, EU AI Act Art. 10
How operators can override, escalation procedures
EU AI Act Art. 14, FCA consumer duty
Version log with change descriptions and re-validation dates
SR 11-7, EU AI Act post-market monitoring
AI disclosure obligations have become a first-class compliance requirement alongside financial reporting, data privacy, and cybersecurity. Organisations must maintain a

that tracks standing annual obligations (SEC 10-K risk factors, NYDFS annual attestation, EU AI Act monitoring reports), periodic obligations (10-Q material AI events, ECOA adverse-action notices), and event-driven obligations (SEC 8-K material incidents, NYDFS 72-hour notification, EU AI Act serious incident reports).
The materiality determination workflow is the critical control point for event-driven disclosures. The SEC 4-business-day clock runs from the

— not discovery — so the internal assessment process must be fast and documented. Legal counsel must be involved before any public filing, but the compliance team should maintain a pre-approved disclosure template and data-collection checklist that can be activated immediately when a potential material AI event is identified.

Model cards and technical documentation are both internal governance artefacts and regulatory evidence. Maintaining them in a version-controlled system (with dated snapshots at each material model change) ensures they are available for regulator examination, conformity assessment, and shareholder disclosure on demand, without last-minute reconstruction that risks inconsistency or error.
